# Australians warned to brace for major inflation hit, as Trump's peace plans unclear

## The US-Israeli war on Iran is threatening to destabilise the global economy.

![Donald Trump and Anthony Albanese loom in a stylised image over a map of Iran and an image of an oil tanker.](https://assets.sbs.com.au/dims4/default/9766c29/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1920x1080+0+0/resize/1280x720!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fsbs-au-brightspot.s3.ap-southeast-2.amazonaws.com%2F21%2Fa2%2F0e5a6a99409db6e59c93d399029d%2Fme-conflict-economic-crisis-header.png&imwidth=1280)

The Prime Minister has warned that the conflict in the Middle East will have serious ramifications for the country. Source: Getty, AAP / Galeanu Mihai / Bloomberg / Gallo Images / Bianca De Marchi / Pool

## in brief

- The war is closing in on its second month, with peace talks appearing to have reached a stalemate.
- Experts have cautioned since the start of the conflict that the longer it drags on, the greater its economic impact.

**Australians could end up paying up to 20 per cent more on everyday items as the war in the Middle East closes in on its second month with no end in sight, experts say.**

The prices of fuel, food, and other basics are expected to rise, feeding inflation and driving cost-of-living pressures even higher as global shipping remains disrupted.

Experts have cautioned since the outbreak of the conflict on 28 February that the longer it drags on, the greater its economic impact will be, with a [recession becoming increasingly likely](https://www.sbs.com.au/news/article/chalmers-reacts-to-imfs-recession-warning-as-he-heads-to-us/wjzey22zx) each passing week.

In its April global analysis, NAB wrote that oil prices over US$120 a barrel are where recession starts to become a real risk, something they say has almost a fifty-fifty chance of happening.

Its worst-case forecast sees the Australian economy contract by 2 per cent, unemployment climb above 9 per cent, and house prices collapse.

Inflation figures from the Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) are due to be released on Wednesday, giving a first look into how the conflict has impacted the Australian economy.

Greg Jericho, chief economist at progressive think tank the Australia Institute, told SBS News that those figures will only capture a limited picture, as fuel prices fluctuate.

"We haven't really seen the big flow through yet into other areas, especially foodstuffs and things like that," Jericho said.

"There doesn't seem to be any short-term prospect of things being resolved, and as a result, I think we can expect to see petrol prices probably start going back up again at some point."

## Recession looming

On Monday, the price of oil climbed once again as US-Iranian negotiations stalled. Benchmark Brent crude futures rose around 2 per cent to a three-week high of US$108 ($150) a barrel in Asia, a level that has stoked inflation worries and prompted analysts to all but price out rate cuts in developed markets this year.

Fossil fuel production and transport, primarily natural gas and oil, have been hampered by the [closure of the Strait of Hormuz](https://www.sbs.com.au/news/article/iran-seizes-two-ships-in-hormuz-wont-reopen-strait-until-us-blockade-remains/419igeek5) by Iran and now the United States.

Iran has also damaged more than 80 LNG and oil facilities in neighbouring Gulf states, which could potentially take years to restore to their previous capacity.

![A front-end loader pours a white, crystalline substance into a processing hopper at an industrial facility, while a forklift and workers manage large bulk bags of the material nearby.](https://assets.sbs.com.au/ca/a5/329a8eee403fb46d09f096cfde69/gettyimages-2270652539.jpg?imwidth=1280)The prices of urea fertiliser — being prepared for shipping at a dock in Yantai, Shandong, China, in this photo — have more than doubled since the war began. Source: Getty / CFOTO / Future Publishing

Fertiliser, industrial chemicals, aluminium, and other products essential to food, technology, and medicines are all impacted by the shutdown in cargo traffic through the narrow waterway.

The average liquid natural gas price for June delivery into northeast Asia was US$16.70 per million British thermal units last week, nearly 61 per cent above pre-war levels.

Goldman Sachs analysts lifted year-end oil price forecasts sharply from US$80 ($111) to US$90 ($125) a barrel for brent crude, assuming Gulf exports normalise by the end of June.

"Non-linear price increases are likely if inventories drop to critically low levels, which we have not seen in the last few decades," they warned in a note, meaning the price could rise substantially if the reduction in oil supply is sustained.

## The Australian response

The RBA is widely expected by major banks to raise interest rates in May, bringing the cash rate to 4.35 per cent, a rate it is not expected to drop below until "mid-to-late" 2027, NAB predicts.

Commonwealth Bank economists have tipped inflation to rise almost a full percentage point for the month, from 3.7 per cent to 4.6 per cent.

The trimmed mean, which excludes more volatile price increases like petrol costs, is expected to climb up from 3.3 to 3.5 per cent, the bank says. Both figures are well above the 2 to 3 per cent band that the RBA targets for inflation.

With the federal budget due in two weeks, Treasurer Jim Chalmers has appeared gloomy on Australia's economic outlook.

Following his recent attendance at the IMF-World Bank Spring Meetings in the US, the treasurer said it was a "really dangerous time" for the global economy.

"The IMF is sounding the alarm on some pretty severe scenarios," Chalmers told reporters in Brisbane.

"Australia is better placed and better prepared than a number of other countries. We won't be spared the fallout from this very substantial economic shock."

![A middle-aged white man with short black and grey hair. He's frowning. ](https://assets.sbs.com.au/01/76/6983fcfd44c2bf3b27185326c5bc/20260225149777244292-original.jpg?imwidth=1280)Treasurer Jim Chalmers has issued a warning that Australians shouldn't expect to escape the economic pain of the Middle Eastern conflict. Source: AAP / Darren England

Jericho suggested that cost-of-living measures would be targeted to relieve pressure on consumers in the upcoming budget, but that a touted 25 per cent increase on natural gas companies would go much further in helping to weather the economic shocks.

"It would be a really missed political as well as economic opportunity," he said of the proposal supported by some crossbenchers, the government has already poured cold water on.

"The government is going to have to stand up and say, 'We've had to make all these massive cuts to the \[National Disability Insurance Scheme\] and all these other things, because we couldn't afford it'".

"And everyone's going to go, 'Well, you could have afforded it. You just chose to keep giving the gas industry a free pass.'"

The prime minister has [cited the gas industry's multi-billion-dollar investments](https://www.sbs.com.au/news/article/labor-say-no-change-on-gas-taxation/u41vnlg92) in extraction as an argument against adding an additional tax.

## Fuel crisis

Since the conflict's outbreak, questions have been raised about Australia's fuel security.

While the government has [now increased domestic petrol reserves from 36 days to 46 days](https://www.sbs.com.au/news/article/australia-now-has-more-fuel-on-hand-than-before-the-war-in-the-middle-east-began/hqtjdckd7), Prime Minister Anthony Albanese told reporters last week that "we're in deeply tumultuous and turbulent global times".

Professor Tina Soliman-Hunter, co-director of the Transforming Energy Markets Research Centre at Macquarie University, told SBS News that the government has very little control over the price of fuel in a privatised economy and should do what it can now to ensure the country does not run dry.

"We're only at tier two \[of the four-tier national fuel security plan\]," she said. "I think we should immediately be put up to tier three. That means being very conservative with our fuel.

"This is not going away. Instead of waiting to react, why don't we be proactive?"

![A petrol station is seen with no petrol available and all pumps out of service](https://assets.sbs.com.au/98/a4/d2e73dc74572b593ecb839abe378/20260325178232920746-original.jpg?imwidth=1280)Australia experienced a wave of service stations without fuel last month, something the government attributed to panic buying. Source: AAP / Dan Himbrechts

US President Donald Trump initially suggested in an interview with the New York Times that the war would last "four to five weeks" and has since variously stated that the conflict would be "wrapped up soon" and that the US is prepared to fight it indefinitely.

Last week, Trump unilaterally extended the two-week ceasefire with Iran, however he cancelled a trip to the Pakistani capital of Islamabad by US envoys for talks over the weekend.

US outlet Axios has reported that Iran wants to make a deal on opening the Strait of Hormuz first while postponing talks on the sticking point of its nuclear capabilities until later.

"I think we need to expect more of the same and a little more tightening of belts," Soliman-Hunter said of the hazy timeline. "I would expect 10 to 20 per cent higher costs."

"The government should be looking at ways to steady the ship, to tell people what to expect and how they're going to be able to guide us."

Australian farmers have already issued [similar warnings about 20 per cent increases to grocery prices](https://www.sbs.com.au/news/article/the-groceries-that-could-rise-20-per-cent-as-farmers-issue-warning/2htpmw692) looming as sowing seasons have suffered.

_— With additional reporting by Reuters and the Australian Associated Press._

# Australia and New Zealand Airlines Struggle with Major Flight Cancellations and Delays, Stranding Hundreds of Passengers

Published on
April 27, 2026

![Passengers waiting at airport with flight delays and cancellations display.](https://www.travelandtourworld.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/Australia-and-New-Zealand-Airlines-Struggle-with-Major-Flight-Cancellations-and-Delays-Stranding-Hundreds-of-Passengers.jpg)

Image generated with Ai

On Sunday, April 26, a significant disruption in air travel across Australia and New Zealand left hundreds of passengers stranded, as major airlines such as Jetstar, Qantas, and Virgin Australia were forced to cancel 34 flights and delay 272 others. This unprecedented wave of cancellations and delays caused widespread chaos at major airports, including Sydney, Melbourne, Brisbane, Wellington, Auckland, and Christchurch. The consequences were felt by both business travelers and holidaymakers, making it one of the most significant disruptions in recent memory for tourists and locals alike.

### Impact on Airports Across Australia and New Zealand

Sydney Airport, Australia’s busiest, was hit the hardest, with 10 flights cancelled and 69 others delayed. Passengers at Sydney were left waiting for hours, with many travelers expressing frustration at the lack of timely updates. Melbourne’s Tullamarine Airport faced 84 delayed flights and additional cancellations, further compounding the travel nightmare for those trying to reach their destinations. Brisbane Airport was similarly affected, with 62 flights experiencing significant delays, disrupting the schedules of both business travelers and vacationers.

The chaos wasn’t confined to Australia. New Zealand’s Auckland Airport saw eight flights cancelled and 44 others delayed, particularly impacting long-haul connecting services. Wellington experienced fewer disruptions, but still faced four flight cancellations. With major airlines like Jetstar and Virgin Australia primarily responsible for the delays, the knock-on effect spread across the region, affecting international and domestic services alike.

### Key Points from the Disruption:

- **34 flights cancelled and 272 delayed across Australia and New Zealand on April 26**
- **Sydney, Melbourne, Brisbane, Auckland, and Wellington airports hit hardest**
- **Business travelers and holidaymakers left stranded or severely delayed**
- **The Middle East conflict and rising fuel prices cited as contributing factors**
- **Airlines such as Jetstar, Virgin Australia, and Qantas bear the brunt of the cancellations**

### Understanding the Root Causes and the Impact on Business and Tourism

While the immediate cause of the disruption is not entirely clear, the broader context of rising fuel prices, exacerbated by the ongoing Middle East conflict, has placed significant pressure on airlines in both Australia and New Zealand. New Zealand’s national carrier, Air New Zealand, had already announced plans to reduce flights in May and June due to soaring fuel costs. Business travelers, in particular, have been affected, with important meetings and corporate events facing delays. In addition, the disruption is creating challenges for tourists hoping to travel to popular destinations, including Sydney’s iconic Opera House or the beautiful beaches of Queensland.

Advertisement

For those looking to travel to New Zealand, disruptions have especially impacted long-haul services, meaning connecting flights to international destinations from Auckland and Wellington were significantly delayed or cancelled.

### Tips for Affected Travelers

If you find yourself caught in this air travel disruption, follow these tips to minimize the impact of delays and cancellations:

Advertisement

Advertisement

1. **Contact Your Airline Immediately**: Airlines may offer rebooking options or compensation. It’s essential to reach out early to secure the best alternatives.
2. **Check for Compensation**: According to government regulations, passengers experiencing significant delays or cancellations may be entitled to compensation. Visit the official website of your airline to learn more about your rights.
3. **Stay Updated via Mobile Apps**: Most airlines and airports now provide real-time updates via mobile apps. Enable notifications to stay in the loop.
4. **Have a Backup Plan**: If your flight is canceled, consider nearby airports or other airlines for rerouting. Be flexible with your travel dates if possible.
5. **Accommodation and Essentials**: If you are stranded overnight, check with your airline about hotel accommodations or vouchers for meals.

### Impact on Tourism and Business Travel

The effects of these disruptions extend beyond mere inconvenience. Tourists planning to visit Australia and New Zealand may face significant delays, with key destinations like the Great Barrier Reef in Australia or Rotorua in New Zealand potentially harder to access. For business travelers, time-sensitive meetings and events are at risk of being postponed or canceled.

Australia and New Zealand’s tourism industries, both major contributors to the regional economy, may see short-term dips in business due to these disruptions. Airlines may also need to adapt to the ongoing effects of rising fuel prices, which could result in further disruptions in the near future.

### Additional Resources for Affected Travelers

- **Australian Aviation Authority** (for real-time flight tracking and passenger information)
- **Air New Zealand** (for detailed information on ongoing flight reductions and cancellations)
- **Travel Insurance**: Consider purchasing or reviewing travel insurance to cover unexpected disruptions and delays.

### Conclusion: Travel Disruptions and What You Can Do

The flight cancellations and delays experienced on April 26 across Australia and New Zealand are a stark reminder of the complexities of air travel in the current global landscape. Business travelers and tourists alike have been caught in a web of delays, with major airlines bearing the brunt of the burden. While the causes of these disruptions are varied, including rising fuel costs and geopolitical tensions, there are steps that travelers can take to ensure that their travel experience remains as smooth as possible.

If you’re planning a trip to Australia or New Zealand in the near future, stay vigilant, stay updated, and ensure you’re prepared for any eventuality. Despite the challenges, both countries remain top destinations for tourism and business alike, with their rich culture, stunning landscapes, and vibrant economies.

# Major Australian construction company collapses with hundreds of jobs at risk

Story by Ashley Nickel

• 2w
•

2 min read

![](<Base64-Image-Removed>)

- **Companies tied to Kwikform entered administration**
- **More than 650 workers are affected**

Several companies connected to one of Australia's largest construction groups have entered administration.

A total of ten companies tied to Kwikform, the Australian arm of South African company Waco International, went into voluntary administration on Monday and Tuesday.

The Sydney-based group traded as Waco Kwikform, Star Scaffolds and United Scaffolding Group.

Its main business was hiring, selling and manufacturing formwork, scaffolding and shoring in 23 locations across Australia and [New Zealand](https://www.dailymail.co.uk/news/new_zealand/index.html), the [Daily Telegraph](https://www.dailytelegraph.com.au/business/new-south-wales/major-australian-construction-group-kwikform-plunges-into-voluntary-administration-after-loss/news-story/035e626b66d80f12e2e8ad54798a3d07) reported.

It's understood more than 650 staff were affected by the decision this week.

Waco Kwikform has been a long-term partner of F1 in [Melbourne](https://www.dailymail.co.uk/news/melbourne/index.html) and provided scaffolding for the John Hunter Hospital in Newcastle.

It is unclear how much the companies owe.

They are overseen by Waco International CEO Stephen Goodburn - aged 62, CFO Dharishan Padiachy - aged 39, and Waco Australasia CEO Michael Els - aged 58.

Damien Pasfield, Melissa Smith, Kare Johnstone and Jason Preston from McGrathNicol have been appointed as administrators.

'Waco Australasia management has been actively pursuing concurrent sales processes for a number of business units in recent months,' they said in a statement.

'While these efforts were promising, current market conditions and delays in transaction timing have made the appointment of Administrators necessary to ensure there is sufficient runway to conclude the processes and maximise value for creditors.'

The companies will continue to trade while administrators work to resolve the debts.

The most recent financial report, seen by the outlet, reported a total of $148.1million in revenue for Waco Kwikform and a $10.54million loss, down from a $9million profit the year prior.

Other entities in the Waco International Group are not affected by the administration.

# IOI Properties to buy Singapore office tower for RM7.7bil

Story by Lee Min Keong

• 1w
•

2 min read

![](<Base64-Image-Removed>)

PETALING JAYA: IOI Properties Group Bhd (IOIPG) is boosting its portfolio of commercial properties in Singapore by acquiring a 46-storey office block from CapitaLand Integrated Commercial Trust (CICT) for a whopping S$2.48 billion (RM7.7 billion).

The proposed acquisition for Asia Square Tower 2 will be done via its wholly owned subsidiary IOI Marina View Pte Ltd and scheduled for completion by the second half of the year.

With this latest acquisition, the property developer will be expanding its unit’s investment assets under management (AUM) in Singapore to S$10 billion (RM31.1 billion).

The transaction is subject to IOI Marina View obtaining a confirmation from Singapore’s tax authority that additional conveyance duty for buyers does not apply, and approval of IOIPG’s shareholders.

The group said this will be an opportunity for IOIPG to strengthen its presence in Singapore’s central business district (CBD), particularly in the prestigious Marina Bay area. The move will enlarge its unit’s portfolio with a total net lettable area (NLA) of 2.57 million sq ft.

“The proposed acquisition reinforces the IOI brand and expands the group’s portfolio of high-quality assets, in line with its strategy to deepen its foothold in Singapore’s prime office market,” it said in a statement today.

Completed in September 2013, Asia Square Tower 2 is located adjacent to the group’s IOI Central Boulevard Towers, and has a total net lettable area of approximately 773,000 sq ft.

Trading in the company’s shares was suspended for an hour from 9am today in conjunction with the announcement. At the midday-break, the shares fell 7 sen or 1.8% to RM3.84, valuing the group at RM21.14 billion.

The shares have surged 45% year to date and 115% in the past one year, touching a new all-time high of RM3.96 last week.

The strong momentum was driven by IOIPG’s proposal to establish a real estate investment trust (REIT) for its retail, office and hotel assets in Malaysia, and potentially in Singapore as well.

Research houses and investors are generally bullish on IOIPG based on the potential of it spinning off some of its Singapore property assets into a REIT across the causeway. The proposed acquisition of Asia Square Tower 2 will only add to the speculation.

Hong Leong Investment Bank said the upcoming REIT listing marks the first step in unlocking value from the group’s Malaysia assets, but the larger re-rating driver lies in its Singapore portfolio, which is over three times larger.

“This is further underpinned by a structural upcycle in the Singapore office market, with tight supply and strong demand,” it said in a note last week.

IOIPG is helmed by billionaire brothers Lee Yeow Chor and Lee Yeow Seng, who hold a controlling stake of about 65.7% through the family vehicles of the late founder and their Lee Shin Cheng.

Yeow Seng leads IOI Properties as the group CEO, while his older brother Yeow Chor runs the affiliated palm oil group IOI Corp Bhd.

# We're not in Australia anymore: Kansas cops jump into action to save kangaroo

"If your Kangaroo wandered off last night ... please let us know."

By[Jon Haworth](https://abcnews.com/author/jon_haworth)

June 30, 2023, 5:11 AM

Animal control officers are used to dealing with a lot of different species but an unusual discovery on Wednesday was a "leap" beyond anything they expected to find.

Police and animal control officers in Edwardsville, Kansas, worked together with the Bonner Springs Police Department on Wednesday night to rescue an injured kangaroo off the side of a major highway.

[Related\\
\\
MORE: Police K-9 dies from heat exhaustion after patrol car malfunctions, unexpectedly shuts off](https://abcnews.com/US/police-9-dies-heat-exhaustion-after-patrol-car/story?id=100063486)

![](https://i.abcnewsfe.com/a/1a14301c-81d0-4616-a132-e2c56bf3db49/kangaroo-embed1_1688115728928_hpEmbed_7x15.jpg)

Police and animal control officers in Edwardsville, Kansas, worked together with the Bonner Springs Police Department on Wednesday, June 28, 2023, to rescue an injured kangaroo off the side of a major highway.

Facebook / Edwardsville Police Department

"Edwardsville Officers and \[Animal Control Officer\] Anthony, with Bonner Springs PD, worked together to capture an injured Kangaroo on I-435," said the Edwardsville Police Department in a statement following the incident.

Authorities were able to capture the injured marsupial and take it in for veterinary care where it is currently being treated to determine the extent of its injuries. The injuries are not believed to be serious, however, and the kangaroo is reportedly eating and drinking according to ABC News' [Kansas City affiliate KMBC](https://www.kmbc.com/article/kangaroo-found-bonner-springs-edwardsville-kansas-i-435/44388105).

[Related\\
\\
MORE: 2 people who allegedly harassed bison calf at national park being hunted by authorities](https://abcnews.com/US/2-people-allegedly-harassed-bison-calf-national-park/story?id=99954505)

![](https://i.abcnewsfe.com/a/f1125ee2-edb6-4a24-9b39-0e3d8d51aaa7/kangaroo-embed2_1688115807509_hpEmbed_3x4.jpg)

Police and animal control officers in Edwardsville, Kansas, worked together with the Bonner Springs Police Department on Wednesday, June 28, 2023, to rescue an injured kangaroo off the side of a major highway.

Facebook / Edwardsville Police Department

The Edwardsville Police Department issued a rather unusual public plea to locate the animal's owners.

"If your Kangaroo wandered off last night, or you may have seen one in the area please let us know," authorities said.

[Related\\
\\
MORE: With only dozens left, South Dakota zoo welcomes litter of 'critically endangered' red wolf pups](https://abcnews.com/US/dozens-left-south-dakota-zoo-welcomes-litter-critically/story?id=99783214)

![](https://i.abcnewsfe.com/a/30575c47-0313-49ea-90be-9220a3189404/kangaroo-embed3_1688115893532_hpEmbed_3x4.jpg)

Police and animal control officers in Edwardsville, Kansas, worked together with the Bonner Springs Police Department on Wednesday, June 28, 2023, to rescue an injured kangaroo off the side of a major highway.

Facebook / Edwardsville Police Department

Just hours later, the joey's owners -- who reportedly run a business featuring exotic animals, according to [KMBC](https://www.kmbc.com/article/kangaroo-found-bonner-springs-edwardsville-kansas-i-435/44388105) \-\- were found and more details were revealed as to how it ended up injured on the side of a major highway.

[![](https://i.abcnewsfe.com/a/c0db23da-df1a-4805-8851-d56d8ec05391/hormuz-1-rt-jef-260427_1777295350524_hpMain_square.jpg?w=208)\\
\\
**Iran live updates: US being 'humiliated' by Iran, German chancellor says**\\
\\
- Apr 27, 11:27 AM](https://abcnews.com/International/live-updates/iran-live-updates-marines-uss-tripoli-seized-iranian/?id=132196152)

[![](https://i.abcnewsfe.com/a/197e4c06-e145-4a89-a70c-35a9e1e48c1f/donald-trump-44-rt-gmh-260425_1777177655046_hpMain_square.jpg?w=208)\\
\\
**'Absolute chaos': Gunman charges WH correspondents' dinner checkpoint**\\
\\
- Apr 26, 12:52 AM](https://abcnews.com/Politics/trump-lady-removed-security-after-incident-white-house/story?id=132387016)

[![](https://i.abcnewsfe.com/a/e34f24cb-af38-42e0-a5c5-2fc29803afee/reiner-gty-jef-260424_1777038565761_hpMain_square.jpg?w=208)\\
\\
**Rob Reiner's son Jake Reiner speaks out in heartbreaking statement**\\
\\
- Apr 24, 10:10 AM](https://abcnews.com/US/rob-reiners-son-jake-reiner-speaks-living-nightmare/story?id=132346792)

The kangaroo allegedly escaped from the owners while it was being transported back from a petting zoo held at a local area library, according to [KMBC](https://www.kmbc.com/article/kangaroo-found-bonner-springs-edwardsville-kansas-i-435/44388105).

[Related\\
\\
MORE: Good Samaritan helping ducks cross busy street killed by teenage driver: Police](https://abcnews.com/US/good-samaritan-helping-ducks-cross-busy-street-killed/story?id=99531576)

![](https://s.abcnews.com/images/US/kangaroo-main_1688115453839_hpMain.jpg)

Police and animal control officers in Edwardsville, Kansas, worked together with the Bonner Springs Police Department on Wednesday, June 28, 2023, to rescue an injured kangaroo off the side of a major highway.

Facebook / Edwardsville Police Department

Kangaroos are native to Australia and New Guinea and even though they have been introduced to other countries, it's almost unheard of to find one outside of a zoo in the United States.

For now, it is unclear when the marsupial will be released from the vet but authorities expect a "hoppy" ending and for it to make a full recovery.

![Australia boosts defense with $860m armored vehicle deal](https://news.az/photos/2026/04/1777269531.webp)

Photo: Bloomberg

+↺−16px

**Australia is expanding its military capabilities with a major new defense purchase, approving more than $800 million in armored vehicle acquisitions as part of a broader buildup in national defense spending.**

The government said it will invest A$1.2 billion (about $860 million) to acquire nearly 300 new Bushmaster protected mobility vehicle units and upgrade existing armored trucks used by the Australian Army, [News.Az](https://news.az/) reports, citing [Bloomberg](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-26/australia-adds-to-weapons-buildup-with-new-armored-vehicle-deal?embedded-checkout=true).

The vehicles will be manufactured by the local arm of Thales, reinforcing [Australia](https://news.az/news/princess-catherine-pays-tribute-to-anzac-war-dead)’s push to strengthen domestic defense production capacity while modernizing its land forces.

Officials described the deal as part of the country’s largest peacetime increase in defense spending, reflecting growing regional security concerns and a broader effort to enhance military readiness.

The new procurement follows a series of recent defense investments as Canberra accelerates modernization of its armed forces, focusing on mobility, protection, and rapid deployment capabilities.

Analysts say the move underscores Australia’s ongoing shift toward a more assertive defense posture amid heightened geopolitical tensions in the Indo-Pacific region.

news.markdown:
#### Nelly Korda is a three-time major champion and back to No.1 in women's golf after her Houston success, while Aussie Hannah Green enjoyed another top-10 finish.

Nelly Korda won The Chevron Championship with a performance worthy of her return to No.1 in women's golf.

Enjoying to a five-stroke lead to start Sunday, Korda was efficient as ever and no one could get closer than four shots at Memorial Park. She closed with a two-under 70 for a five-shot victory to capture the third major of her career.

The victory was her 17th on the LPGA Tour and 21st worldwide, and it was enough for the 27-year-old American star to move back to the top of the women's world ranking for the first time since August.

Korda not only won wire-to-wire, it was never really a contest. She hit 5-iron to five feet for birdie on her 16th hole of Thursday's opening round to take the lead and never trailed again.

Twitter Embed

> She does it again 🙌 The winning moment for 3x major champion, Nelly Korda [pic.twitter.com/LomOivjGbQ](https://t.co/LomOivjGbQ)
>
> — LPGA (@LPGA) [April 26, 2026](https://twitter.com/LPGA/status/2048515987876577552?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw)

As easy as Korda made it look, it felt like a struggle.

"That was a hard weekend," she said.

"Honestly, having that big of a lead, it's not easy. It was definitely one of the hardest things I've had to do mentally ... Just happy to get it done."

She celebrated in the best manner possible — a cannon ball into the pool built to the right of the 18th green to keep with the tradition at this major that dates to 1988 when the winner jumped into Poppie's Pond at Mission Hills in the California desert.

Korda joined Juli Inkster (1989) and Amy Alcott (1991) as the only players in the last 50 years to win LPGA majors when leading by multiple shots after each round.

About the only drama in the final hour — all weekend, really — was whether Korda could break Dottie Pepper's 72-hole scoring record that has stood since 1999.

She finished at 18-under 270, one short of Pepper's record at Mission Hills.

China's Ruoning Yin (69) and Patty Tavatanakit (70) of Thailand tied for second, while Hannah Green (68) was the best-placed Australian in joint seventh at six under.

"Overall I'm pretty happy with this as my first major performance (of 2026)," Green said.

"I'm really excited to go to Riviera, obviously being in California, but also Hazeltine National, somewhere obviously very close to my heart.

"I haven't been there since I won (the 2019 PGA Championship), so it'll be really strange and cool to get there."

Compatriots Cassie Porter (74) finished one under, Minjee Lee (73) one over and Karis Davidson (76) five over.

Korda won $US1.35 million ($A1.89 million) for a victory that puts her back as the best in women's golf without any debate. She won seven times in 2024, including a record-tying five in a row she capped off with a victory at The Chevron when it was at Carlton Woods.

This was the first time the LPGA's opening major was held at Memorial Park, a big golf course that had been redesigned for the Houston Open on the PGA Tour.

The Australian Federal Police has arrested and charged what appears to be the first person linked to a wave of recent suspected mortgage fraud, alleging he is associated with a criminal money laundering syndicate involved in illicit tobacco, drug importation and onshore scam networks.

Melbourne accountant Qi Gao, also known as Andy Gao, was arrested and charged on March 31. He was granted bail to appear in the Magistrates’ Court in July, and faces a maximum penalty of 27 years in prison.

![](https://static.ffx.io/images/$zoom_0.322%2C$multiply_3%2C$ratio_1.5%2C$width_756%2C$x_47%2C$y_195/t_crop_custom/c_scale%2Cw_620%2Cq_88%2Cf_auto/609d452bf46cfaa032c9fa6d7ad46f944f02549b)

Andy Gao outside his offices in Melbourne on Monday. Eamon Gallagher

The charges follow broader allegations that $3 billion in estimated fraud has been linked to multiple individuals and businesses across the country’s $2.4 trillion mortgage market this year.

The heightened levels of suspected mortgage fraud – which include the use of artificial intelligence-created documents and shell companies to obtain finance – have authorities fearing the proceeds of crime are being washed through the banking system.

Investigations by banks into their mortgage books have entangled a string of small companies and individuals including accountants, lawyers and real estate agents in the potential fraud activity.

Advertisement

Those loan referral sources, alongside mortgage brokers, feed customers into banks and other lenders in exchange for commission payments.

Gao, through his firm PGY Accounting + Business Solutions, was allegedly involved in referring loans to mortgage brokers, who were submitting applications to banks, a person with knowledge of the arrangement said on the condition of anonymity.

Gao was not, however, a referrer through Commonwealth Bank’s introducer program, after the country’s largest home loan lender [reported several accountants and two mortgage brokers](https://www.afr.com/companies/financial-services/cba-refers-brokers-accountants-to-police-over-massive-loan-fraud-20260303-p5o74y) to police and the corporate regulator earlier this year, they said.

The AFP confirmed the arrest of 42-year-old Gao. He was charged with one count of knowingly dealing in the proceeds of general crime for money or property worth more than $100,000, and one count of committing an indictable offence for the benefit of a criminal organisation.

“It is alleged the man participated in a money laundering syndicate linked to illicit tobacco, illicit drug importations and onshore scam networks targeting Australian victims,” an AFP spokesman said. “As the matter is before the courts, no further comment will be made.”

Gao declined to comment on Monday. His lawyer, Doogue + George partner Amelia Ramsay, also declined to comment.

Advertisement

PGY lists Gao as a founder and senior manager. The firm says Gao spent 10 years at a major accounting firm in Australia and founded PGY in 2018 with a business partner. The firm specialises in assisting private clients build their wealth and helping small and medium-sized businesses expand.

Gao’s profile says he is a member of CPA Australia and the Institute of Public Accountants.

_The Australian Financial Review_ revealed earlier this year that CBA had found loan irregularities and potential fraud within its mortgage portfolio.

Some of the issues were linked to mortgage brokers while others were associated with referral channels, where accountants, lawyers and real estate agents can refer home loan customers to the bank for a commission.

CBA identified [more than $1 billion in potential questionable](https://www.afr.com/companies/financial-services/cba-probes-1b-in-suspected-fraudulent-home-loans-calls-in-police-20260223-p5o4mc) activity, which also triggered the involvement of financial crimes regulator Austrac.

Reviews conducted by a string of other banks have now uncovered about $3 billion in suspect mortgages across the sector, after Austrac ordered 10 lenders to share data on the source of potential fraudulent activity.

Advertisement

Gao’s arrest is separate to those police have made that are allegedly related to the so-called Penthouse Syndicate.

That group, as first reported by _The_ _Sydney Morning Herald_ and _The Age_ last year, allegedly defrauded National Australia Bank of around $150 million by acquiring home and business loans, including for luxury property.

Last week, [NSW Police charged a lawyer](https://www.afr.com/companies/financial-services/nsw-police-arrest-lawyer-allegedly-at-centre-of-bank-fraud-syndicate-20260422-p5zq1u) with helping to facilitate a multimillion-dollar fraud and money laundering syndicate under Strike Force Myddleton, which has been investigating the syndicate since 2024.

Police suspect the Penthouse Syndicate has defrauded all four of the country’s major banks by at least $300 million, amid growing concerns that washing illicit funds through real estate is a systemic problem.

Separately, the fallout from the mortgage fraud issue has continued to ripple across the industry, including to aggregators that provide compliance services and lender relationships to brokers.

Finsure, an aggregator, last week terminated an agreement with Sydney-based firm Hai Money after a string of compliance concerns were raised by banks about brokers operating within the latter’s network.

Advertisement

Hai terminated 14 brokers, including alleged Penthouse-linked former banker Andrew Hu, after National Australia Bank suspended accreditation following concerns about their conduct.

Hai’s founder Bruce Li said an independent report by PwC into the firm’s compliance processes found “no evidence of systemic broker misconduct” across its network of 200 brokers.

“Finsure’s actions in terminating the agreement were hasty, disproportionate and grossly unfair,” Li said.

### Claire Harvey

Editorial Director

Claire Harvey is Editorial Director of The Australian and hosts the daily news podcast The Front. She has been a foreign correspondent, deputy editor, columnist and reporter for over 30 years. Claire is a leading innovator in digital storytelling.

![](https://dsf.newscorpaustralia.com/taus/wp-content/uploads/sites/79/2026/02/Journalist-images_280x280_Caroline-Overington.png)

### Caroline Overington

Literary Editor

Caroline Overington, a celebrated Australian writer and journalist, is a two-time Walkley winner. She's authored 16 books, hosted true crime documentaries, and topped Audible charts with her thrillers. As literary editor for The Weekend Australian Review, she now champions fellow writers.

![](https://dsf.newscorpaustralia.com/taus/wp-content/uploads/sites/79/2026/02/Journalist-images_280x280_Simon-Benson.png)

### Simon Benson

Political Editor

Simon Benson, an award-winning journalist, has been The Australian's Political Editor and National Affairs Editor. He was also the Daily Telegraph’s NSW political editor and led the NSW Press Gallery.

**8:00PM** Monday, April 27th, 2026

# Australia is one of the least well-placed developed nations to handle oil crisis as inflation weighs on economy, major bank warns

Australia is one of the “least well-placed” developed nations to handle the oil crisis as sticky inflation weighs on the economy, a major bank has warned.

[![James Harrison](https://resources.news.com.au/author-profiles/bfa66cd2-249d-47f8-b654-e8f5960a6495.jpeg)](https://www.skynews.com.au/the-team/james-harrison) [James Harrison](https://www.skynews.com.au/the-team/james-harrison) Business Reporter

2 min read

April 27, 2026 - 3:35PM

[Sky News Australia](https://www.skynews.com.au/)[24 Comments](https://www.skynews.com.au/business/finance/australia-is-one-of-the-least-wellplaced-developed-nations-to-handle-oil-crisis-as-inflation-weighs-on-economy-major-bank-warns/news-story/89a433f667d2a9b9445b9abc87992740#comments)

03:38

X

![Next video thumbnail](https://content.api.news/v3/images/bin/841222e365652452dfc62487543d13d8)

Budget creeping up as ‘economic strife’ continues for Australia

Sky News host Chris Kenny says Australia is in “economic strife” as the budget creeps up.

moreless

Toggle full description view

Australia’s poor productivity, susceptibility to inflation and tight jobs market make it one of the “least well-placed” developed nations to handle the oil crisis, a major bank has warned.

HSBC’s chief economist Paul Bloxham said Australia’s economy has been operating beyond its capacity, making economic growth inflationary.

Low productivity growth forces prices up when the economy grows, he argued, flagging concerns about the tight jobs market.

“In our view, this combination makes Australia is one of the least well-placed developed economies to deal with the inflation shock that is arriving,” Mr Bloxham said.

Inflation in the 12 months to February was 3.7 per cent. This period was not impacted by the fuel crisis.

![HSBC has warned that Australia is one of the ](https://content.api.news/v3/images/bin/c5d67ccdade2949da542de2153e3c11a)

HSBC has warned that Australia is one of the "least-well placed" developed nations to handle the fuel crisis. Picture: NewsWire / John Appleyard

The unemployment rate remains near historic lows at 4.3 per cent, which can put pressure on wages inflation.

Mr Bloxham said inflation before the oil shock is the main challenge for the Reserve Bank of Australia.

“Inflation was already too high, even before the Middle East conflict energy price shock,” he said.

Mr Bloxham said the March inflation figures - due on Wednesday - will confirm that both headline inflation and the trimmed mean figure are “well above” the RBA’s 2-3 per cent target band.

“And this is before the full effect of the fuel price shock arrives,” he said.

Getting inflation as close to target as often as possible can best place Australia to weather another unpredictable shock, Mr Bloxham said.

03:28

X

![Next video thumbnail](https://content.api.news/v3/images/bin/1f2b89b102e4af68dca6da323c160a56)

Jim Chalmers under fire for ‘blaming’ America on his reckless spending

Sky News host Danica De Giorgio lashes out at Treasurer Jim Chalmers for his handling of the economy amid the Iran war.

moreless

Toggle full description view

He also attacked the [RBA’s narrow path tactic](https://www.skynews.com.au/business/finance/rbas-narrow-path-rates-approach-not-the-right-strategy-eq-economics-warren-hogan-declares-after-inflation-lifts/news-story/4748bcf83c8dc19e630691d04023992b) – where inflation returns to target while the economy grows and unemployment remains low – as it failed to kill high inflation.

“With the RBA having taken the slow road to getting inflation back to target - down a 'narrow pathway' that they eventually fell out of - the economy is now not very well placed to deal with the next shock,” Mr Bloxham said.

Meanwhile, GDP lifted 2.6 per cent throughout 2025 – a substantial increase compared to the post-pandemic fall.

Yet Mr Bloxham warned that Australia would need to endure an economic slump to get inflation under control.

“We expect the Australian economy will need to have a downturn to get inflation to fall back towards the RBA's target,” he said.

“The only real questions, in our view, are: how big that downturn will be and what drives it.”

### Stream Ross Greenwood's leading business coverage on SkyNews.com.au

![](https://multitools.newscdn.com.au/multitools/factbox/content/1714097318070/SNAPP-Business-Now_4oZkxV9SH.jpg)

Join Ross Greenwood as he provides comprehensive business and finance news on Business Now, weekdays at 4.30pm AEDT. Stream live or on-demand with a **[SkyNews.com.au Streaming Subscription](https://www.skynews.com.au/subscribe/news/1/?sourceCode=SNWEB_MPS116_STM&utm_source=skynews&utm_medium=owneddisplay&utm_campaign=stream&content=inarticle)**. Already subscribed? **[Watch it here](https://www.skynews.com.au/stream/news-shows/business-now)**.

_T&Cs apply. Content accessible in Australia only._

Inflation is expected to lift to upwards of five per cent for March as the oil shock takes its toll.

This is likely to correspond with an interest rate rise next month, as economists are pricing in a hike for the May meeting.

Another increase, which would mark the third consecutive hike this year, will likely bring the cash rate back to 4.35 per cent.

This is where rates were held for almost a year and a half to stamp out post-pandemic inflation.

However, trimmed mean inflation only fell below three per cent in June last year before rising again, casting doubt on that cash rate's effectiveness.
